title: The Philosophical Theory of the State
description: The Philosophical Theory of the State — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "关于国家的哲学理论" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: Table of Contents
memes.description: 'Complete table of contents for The Philosophical Theory of the State'

memes.label: America
memes.description: 'America and the United States as discussed by Bosanquet—an example of modern federal republicanism and a distinctive development of the nation-state form.'

relations.label: Chapter X: The State Considered as an Idea

memes.label: Analytic Psychology
memes.description: 'G.F. Stoutʼs _Analytic Psychology_ (1896), an influential work on the structure of mind and apperception cited by Bosanquet in his account of the individual mind.'

relations.label: Chapter II: The State as a Psychical Fact

memes.label: Aristotle
memes.description: 'Aristotle (384–322 BCE), Greek philosopher whose political naturalism and concept of the polis as prior to the individual inform Bosanquetʼs organic theory of the state.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State
Chapter XI: The State and the Social Organism

memes.label: Association
memes.description: 'The relation between the state and other forms of human association (family, church, trade union, corporation)—Bosanquetʼs argument that the state is not merely one association among others but their rational completion.'

relations.label: Chapter VII: The State and Other Associations
Chapter VIII: The State and the Individual

memes.label: Auguste Comte
memes.description: 'Auguste Comte (1798–1857), French philosopher and founder of sociology (which he first called "social physics"), whose positivist conception of social science is a defining influence on modern sociological method and a critical reference point for Bosanquetʼs idealist theory of the state.'
aliases: Isidore Auguste Marie François Xavier Comte

relations.label: Chapter II: The State as a Psychical Fact

memes.label: Authority
memes.description: 'The nature and justification of political authority—Bosanquetʼs argument that the authority of the state rests not on force or consent but on its character as the embodiment of the general will.'

relations.label: Chapter IV: The Authority of the State

memes.label: Autonomy
memes.description: 'The Kantian concept of moral self-legislation and its transformation in Bosanquetʼs idealist theory, where individual autonomy is realized through participation in the rational state.'

relations.label: Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power
Chapter V: Liberty and the General Will

memes.label: Baruch Spinoza
memes.description: 'Baruch Spinoza (1632–1677), Dutch philosopher of Portuguese-Jewish descent, whose pantheistic metaphysics and political philosophy are invoked by Bosanquet as an example of the profound unity of thought and life in the great philosophers.'

relations.label: Chapter I: Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: Bernard Bosanquet
memes.description: "Bernard Bosanquet (1848–1923), English idealist philosopher and author of _The Philosophical Theory of the State_ (1899), the foundational text of this wiki, as well as works on logic, aesthetics, and social philosophy."

memes.description: "The rise of the modern theory of the state and its relation to Greek political philosophy"

memes.description: "The relation of the state to other social associations"

memes.description: "The ethical relation between the state and the individual"

memes.description: "The connection between the state and moral ideals"

memes.label: Citizenship
memes.description: The status of membership in a political community, grounded in recognition of law as one's own will and resting on the organic unity of the social whole.

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State
Chapter VIII: The State and the Individual
Chapter IX: The State in Relation to Morality

memes.label: The Closed Commercial State
memes.description: Fichte's 1800 treatise advocating state control of foreign trade and economic regulation, discussed by Bosanquet as an early form of state socialism.

memes.label: Coercion
memes.description: For Bosanquet, Coercion by the State is justified not as mere force but as the rational self-assertion of the community — the means by which the common will overcomes particular resistance to secure the conditions of freedom for all.

relations.label: Chapter III: The Paradox of Self-Government
Chapter VIII: The State and Coercion

memes.label: Common Good
memes.description: 'The good shared by all members of a political community, which Bosanquet identifies as the object of the general will and the foundation of legitimate state authority.'

relations.label: Chapter V: Liberty and the General Will

memes.label: Community
memes.description: For Bosanquet, Community is the fundamental form of human association — a shared life of minds that precedes and sustains all political institutions, and in which alone the individual finds full self-realisation.

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State
Chapter III: The Paradox of Self-Government

memes.label: Comparative Politics
memes.description: 'Edward A. Freemanʼs _Comparative Politics_ (1873), an early application of the comparative method to the study of political institutions, cited by Bosanquet.'

relations.label: Chapter II: The State as a Psychical Fact

memes.label: Consent
memes.description: Bosanquet critiques the consent theory of political obligation — the idea that the State's authority rests on the express or tacit agreement of individuals — as inadequate to explain the real moral bonds that constitute political community.

relations.label: Chapter IV: The Theory of the State as a Moral Organism

memes.label: Du Contrat Social
memes.description: 'Rousseauʼs _Du Contrat Social_ (1762), the foundational text of modern democratic theory and the most important philosophical source for Bosanquetʼs idealist theory of the state.'

relations.label: Chapter IV: The Authority of the State

memes.label: David George Ritchie
memes.description: 'David George Ritchie (1853–1903), Scottish philosopher and author of _Natural Rights_ (1895), whose critique of natural rights theory is cited by Bosanquet as an important contribution to idealist political philosophy.'

relations.label: Chapter I: Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: De la Division du Travail Social
memes.description: De la Division du Travail Social (1893) by Émile Durkheim — a foundational work of academic sociology; Bosanquet cites its investigations on population density and the division of labour.
aliases: The Division of Labour in Society

memes.label: England
memes.description: 'England as discussed by Bosanquet—the national context of British idealism and the tradition of political thought from which his own theory emerges.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: Ethical Studies
memes.description: 'F.H. Bradleyʼs _Ethical Studies_ (1876), a major work of British idealist ethics developing the theory of self-realization and my station and its duties.'

relations.label: Chapter IX: The State in Relation to Morality

memes.label: Europe
memes.description: 'Europe as the historical context for the development of the modern nation-state and political philosophy discussed by Bosanquet.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: Evolution and Ethics
memes.description: 'T.H. Huxleyʼs _Evolution and Ethics_ (1893), the Romanes Lecture arguing that ethical progress is opposed to rather than continuous with natural evolution.'

relations.label: Chapter VI: The Institutions of the General Will

memes.label: F.H. Bradley
memes.description: 'F.H. Bradley (1846–1924), British idealist philosopher whose Ethical Studies influenced Bosanquetʼs theory of the self and its relation to the social whole.'

memes.label: Family
memes.description: For Bosanquet, the Family is the primordial ethical institution — the first form of community in which the individual will finds a non-alien object, and the model for understanding how the State transcends mere contract.

relations.label: Chapter VI: The State and the General Will
Chapter X: Institutions — Property and the Family

memes.label: Force
memes.description: Bosanquet distinguishes between mere physical force and the rational force of social institutions, arguing that force is inherent in the State but is transformed from brute power into legitimate authority through its role in maintaining the conditions of freedom.

relations.label: Chapter III: The Paradox of Self-Government

memes.label: France
memes.description: 'France as discussed by Bosanquet—the context of Rousseau and Revolutionary political thought, and a major source of modern democratic ideas.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: Franklin Henry Giddings
memes.description: American sociologist (1855–1931), author of Principles of Sociology; Bosanquet critiques the teleological tendency in his sociological method.

memes.label: French Revolution
memes.description: 'The French Revolution (1789–1799), which Bosanquet discusses as a pivotal moment in the development of modern political consciousness and the practical application of Rousseauʼs theory of the general will.'

memes.label: G.W.F. Hegel
memes.description: 'G.W.F. Hegel (1770–1831), German idealist philosopher whose concepts of objective spirit, the rational state, and the dialectic of individual and community are foundational to Bosanquetʼs political philosophy.'

relations.label: Chapter IX: The State and the Individual
Chapter X: The State Considered as an Idea

memes.label: General Will
memes.description: 'The general will (volonté générale) is the will of a political community directed toward the common good—a foundational concept in Rousseauʼs political philosophy and central to Bosanquetʼs theory of the state.'

relations.label: Chapter IV: The Authority of the State
Chapter V: Liberty and the General Will
Chapter VIII: The State and the Individual

memes.label: G.F. Stout
memes.description: G.F. Stout (1860–1944), British psychologist and philosopher whose Analytic Psychology Bosanquet draws on for the theory of mental control and proportional systems in social organisation.

memes.label: Germany
memes.description: 'Germany as discussed by Bosanquet—the philosophical home of German idealism and the context of Hegelʼs political philosophy.'

relations.label: Chapter IX: The State in Relation to Morality

memes.label: Giambattista Vico
memes.description: Italian philosopher of history (1668–1744), author of New Science; Bosanquet sees him as a precursor to the modern science of society.

memes.label: Ancient Greece
memes.description: 'Ancient Greece, especially the Greek city-state (polis), as discussed by Bosanquet as the originating context of Western political philosophy.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: Henry Maine
memes.description: British jurist and legal historian (1822–1888), author of Ancient Law (1861); Bosanquet engages with his theory of the origin of criminal law.

memes.label: Henry Sidgwick
memes.description: Henry Sidgwick (1838–1900), British philosopher and ethicist whose Methods of Ethics Bosanquet engages with on the simplification of ethical theory and the relation between individual and social good.

memes.label: Herbert Spencer
memes.description: 'Herbert Spencer (1820–1903), English philosopher whose evolutionary organicism Bosanquet criticizes for reducing social life to biological determinism.'

relations.label: Chapter XI: The State and the Social Organism

memes.label: Holland
memes.description: 'Holland (the Netherlands) as referenced by Bosanquet—a historical example in the development of international law and republican government.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: Immanuel Kant
memes.description: 'Immanuel Kant (1724–1804), German philosopher whose moral philosophy—particularly the concept of autonomy—informs Bosanquetʼs theory of liberty and the rational will (003-049).'

relations.label: Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power

memes.label: "Index"
memes.description: "Index of topics and names"

memes.label: India
memes.description: 'India as referenced by Bosanquet—a peripheral example in the comparative study of political institutions and civilizations.'

relations.label: Chapter II: The State as a Psychical Fact

memes.label: Individuality
memes.description: In Bosanquet's theory, Individuality is not the mere distinctness of persons but the rich self-development achieved through participation in the common life of the community and State.

relations.label: Chapter III: The Paradox of Self-Government

memes.label: Institution
memes.description: 'The institutional embodiment of the general will—Bosanquetʼs analysis of how political institutions give concrete form to the rational will of the community.'

relations.label: Chapter VI: The Institutions of the General Will

memes.label: Introduction to Political Science
memes.description: Introduction to Political Science by J.R. Seeley (1896) — a foundational text of academic political science; Bosanquet cites its definition of liberty as illustrative of the individualist view he contests.

memes.label: Italy
memes.description: 'Italy as discussed by Bosanquet—referenced as a distinctive voice in political thought and as an example of national unification.'

relations.label: Chapter X: The State Considered as an Idea

memes.label: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
memes.description: 'Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712–1778), Genevan philosopher whose concept of the general will, developed in _Du Contrat Social_ (1762), is the single most important philosophical foundation for Bosanquetʼs idealist theory of the state.'

relations.label: Chapter IV: The Authority of the State
Chapter V: Liberty and the General Will

memes.label: Jeremy Bentham
memes.description: 'Jeremy Bentham (1748–1832), English philosopher and founder of utilitarianism, whose principle of utility Bosanquet critiques as an inadequate foundation for political obligation.'

relations.label: Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power

memes.label: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
memes.description: 'J.G. Fichte (1762–1814), German idealist philosopher whose ethical nationalism influenced the tradition in which Bosanquet worked.'

relations.label: Chapter X: The State Considered as an Idea

memes.label: John Austin
memes.description: 'John Austin (1790–1859), English legal philosopher whose command theory of law—law as the command of the sovereign backed by sanctions—is critically examined by Bosanquet as an instance of the external, non-philosophical view of law.'

relations.label: Chapter X: The Nature of Law

memes.label: John Locke
memes.description: 'John Locke (1632–1704), English philosopher whose theory of natural rights and limited government represents the liberal tradition that Bosanquet both acknowledges and criticizes.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: J.R. Seeley
memes.description: J.R. Seeley (1834–1895), British historian and author of Introduction to Political Science, whose definition of government Bosanquet critically examines in developing his own theory of the state.

memes.label: John Stuart Mill
memes.description: 'John Stuart Mill (1806–1873), English philosopher whose essay On Liberty is a central text of the liberal tradition that Bosanquet critically engages.'

relations.label: Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power

memes.label: Law
memes.description: 'The nature and function of law in Bosanquetʼs idealist theory—law as the expression of the general will and the concrete form of rational freedom, not a mere command backed by force.'

relations.label: Chapter II: The State as a Psychical Fact
Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power
Chapter VIII: The State and the Individual

memes.label: Lectures and Essays
memes.description: 'William Wallaceʼs _Lectures and Essays on Natural Theology and Ethics_ (1898), posthumously published lectures cited by Bosanquet for their account of the ethical self.'

memes.label: Leviathan
memes.description: 'Hobbesʼs _Leviathan_ (1651), the foundational text of modern sovereignty theory, which Bosanquet critically engages and transforms through the concept of the general will.'
aliases: Leviathan or the Matter Forme and Power of a Commonwealth

relations.label: Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power

memes.label: Liberty
memes.description: 'The nature and conditions of liberty as analyzed in Bosanquetʼs idealist theory of the state—distinguishing natural liberty from civil liberty and arguing that true freedom is realized through the general will.'

relations.label: Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power
Chapter IV: The Authority of the State
Chapter V: Liberty and the General Will

memes.label: Majority Rule
memes.description: Bosanquet critiques the principle of majority rule as an inadequate foundation for political authority, arguing that it reduces the general will to a mere aggregation of individual preferences rather than a genuine rational unity.

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern State

memes.label: The Man versus the State
memes.description: The Man versus the State (1884) by Herbert Spencer — four essays against the expansion of state functions; Bosanquet uses it as a representative expression of the individualistic theory of the state which his idealist theory of the general will seeks to overcome.

memes.label: Montesquieu
memes.description: 'Montesquieu (1689–1755), French political philosopher whose analysis of the spirit of laws (006-076) and forms of government informed Bosanquetʼs understanding of political institutions.'

relations.label: Chapter VI: The Institutions of the General Will

memes.label: Morality
memes.description: 'The relation between morality and the state in Bosanquetʼs idealist philosophy—the state as the embodiment of the ethical idea and the reconciliation of individual morality with social institutions.'

relations.label: Chapter IX: The State in Relation to Morality

memes.label: Nation
memes.description: In Bosanquet's philosophical theory, the nation is a natural community of shared culture and consciousness that precedes and informs the State as a higher rational unity.

relations.label: Chapter II: The Conception of Law and the State

memes.label: Natural Right
memes.description: 'The doctrine that individuals possess inherent rights by virtue of their nature, prior to and independent of the state—critically examined by Bosanquet in his idealist theory of political obligation.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State
Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power
Chapter VIII: The State and the Individual

memes.label: Natural Rights
memes.description: Natural Rights by David George Ritchie (1894) — a critical examination of the theory of natural rights; Bosanquet cites it as a key work in the late nineteenth-century reconsideration of rights discourse.

memes.label: New Science
memes.description: Scienza Nuova (1725/1744) by Giambattista Vico — a pioneering work in the philosophy of history; Bosanquet identifies it as an early attempt to inaugurate the science of society.

memes.label: Obligation
memes.description: In Bosanquet's theory, Obligation — especially political obligation — is not an external imposition but the rational necessity inherent in the will's own nature, grounded in the common good that the State embodies.

relations.label: Chapter III: The Paradox of Self-Government

memes.label: Social Organism
memes.description: 'The analogy between the state and a living organism—Bosanquetʼs qualified defense of the organic theory against mechanistic individualism and his critique of Herbert Spencerʼs version.'

relations.label: Chapter XI: The State and the Social Organism

memes.label: Patrick Geddes
memes.description: Scottish biologist, sociologist, and town planner (1854–1932); Bosanquet references his concept of Regional Survey and his observations on social parasitism and degeneration.

memes.label: Phenomenology of Mind
memes.description: Hegel's Phänomenologie des Geistes (1807), described by Bosanquet as "perhaps the greatest of his works" and foundational to the post-Kantian theory of freedom and the modern spirit.

relations.label: The Philosophical Theory of the State

memes.label: Philosophy of Mind
memes.description: 'Hegelʼs _Philosophy of Mind_ (1817, trans. Wallace 1894), the third part of the _Encyclopedia_, containing Hegelʼs mature theory of subjective and objective spirit.'

relations.label: Chapter IX: The State in Relation to Morality

memes.label: Philosophy of Right
memes.description: 'Hegelʼs _Philosophy of Right_ (1821), the systematic culmination of German idealist political philosophy and a major influence on Bosanquetʼs theory of the state.'

relations.label: Chapter IX: The State in Relation to Morality

memes.label: Physics and Politics
memes.description: 'Walter Bagehotʼs _Physics and Politics_ (1872), applying evolutionary ideas to the study of political development and nation-formation, cited by Bosanquet.'

relations.label: Chapter II: The State as a Psychical Fact

memes.label: Plato
memes.description: 'Plato (c. 428–348 BCE), Athenian philosopher whose theory of the ideal state and the rule of reason is a foundational source for the idealist political tradition that culminates in Bosanquet.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: Political Economy
memes.description: 'Classical political economy as discussed by Bosanquet in relation to his theory of the state and the distinction between state and civil society.'

relations.label: Chapter VII: The State and Other Associations

memes.label: Political Obligation
memes.description: 'The moral duty to obey the law and support the state—Bosanquetʼs idealist account grounding political obligation in the rational will and the common good rather than consent or fear.'

relations.label: Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power
Chapter VI: The Institutions of the General Will
Chapter VIII: The State and the Individual

memes.label: Politics
memes.description: 'Aristotleʼs _Politics_ (c. 350 BCE), the foundational text of political naturalism and a key source for Bosanquetʼs organic theory of the state.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: Preface
memes.description: "Preface by Bernard Bosanquet to The Philosophical Theory of the State (1899)"

memes.label: The Principles of Logic
memes.description: F.H. Bradley's 1883 treatise on logic, cited by Bosanquet in his critique of explanatory method in social philosophy.

memes.label: Principles of Political Obligation
memes.description: 'T.H. Greenʼs _Lectures on the Principles of Political Obligation_ (1886), a foundational text of British idealism frequently cited by Bosanquet.'
aliases: Lectures on the Principles of Political Obligation

relations.label: Chapter VIII: The State and the Individual

memes.label: Principles of Sociology
memes.description: The Principles of Sociology by Franklin Henry Giddings (1896) — an early American sociological treatise; Bosanquet cites its analysis of mechanical conditions of modern city life.

memes.label: Property
memes.description: In Bosanquet's theory, Property is a right created and guaranteed by the State, not a natural right prior to society — it is the external sphere of individual freedom recognised by the common will.

relations.label: Chapter IV: Natural Right and the State
Chapter X: Institutions — Property and the Family

memes.label: Prussia
memes.description: 'Prussia as discussed by Bosanquet—the state that Hegel is often accused of idealizing, treated by Bosanquet as a case study in the relation between philosophy and actual political institutions.'

relations.label: Chapter IX: The State in Relation to Morality

memes.label: Punishment
memes.description: 'The philosophical justification of state punishment—Bosanquetʼs idealist theory that punishment reconciles the criminal with their own rational will as expressed in the general will.'

relations.label: Chapter VIII: The State and the Individual

memes.label: Real Will
memes.description: 'The distinction between the actual will (what one happens to want) and the real will (what one would will if fully rational)—central to Bosanquetʼs theory of political obligation and liberty.'

relations.label: Chapter IV: The Authority of the State
Chapter V: Liberty and the General Will

memes.label: Reason
memes.description: In Bosanquet's idealist political philosophy, reason is the governing principle of political life — the capacity through which individuals transcend mere appetite and participate in the rational order of the State.

relations.label: Chapter IV: The Theory of the State as a Moral Organism

memes.label: The Reformation
memes.description: 'The Protestant Reformation (16th century), discussed by Bosanquet as a key moment in the emergence of modern political consciousness and the development of the theory of individual rights.'

memes.label: Representation
memes.description: In Bosanquet's theory, Representation is not the mechanical delegation of individual preferences but the symbolic embodiment of the community's rational will in institutions and persons that "stand for" the common life.

memes.label: Republic
memes.description: 'Platoʼs _Republic_ (c. 375 BCE), the founding text of Western political philosophy—its account of justice, the ideal polis, and the rule of reason establishes the tradition culminating in Bosanquet.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: Rights
memes.description: 'Bosanquetʼs idealist theory of rights—rights are not pre-political possessions of individuals but positions recognized and maintained by the state as the system of conditions for rational self-realization.'

relations.label: Chapter IV: The Authority of the State
Chapter VIII: The State and the Individual

memes.label: Ancient Rome
memes.description: 'Ancient Rome as discussed by Bosanquet—a key example in the historical development of political and legal institutions from the ancient world.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State
Chapter IV: The Authority of the State

memes.label: Second Treatise of Government
memes.description: 'Lockeʼs _Second Treatise of Government_ (1689), the classic statement of liberal political philosophy based on natural rights and consent—a position Bosanquet criticizes from an idealist standpoint.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: Self-Government
memes.description: 'The principle that citizens should be governed by laws they have a share in making—a paradox that Bosanquet resolves through the concept of the general will and the real will.'

relations.label: Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power
Chapter IV: The Authority of the State
Chapter V: Liberty and the General Will

memes.label: Social Contract
memes.description: 'The theory that political obligation derives from a hypothetical agreement among individuals to form a political society—analyzed and defended by Rousseau and critically examined by Bosanquet.'

relations.label: Chapter IV: The Authority of the State

memes.label: Society
memes.description: In Bosanquet's theory, Society is the living whole of human relations — an organic unity more real than its individual members — yet distinct from the State as the sphere of particular interests and economic exchange.

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: Socrates
memes.description: 'Socrates (c. 470–399 BCE), Athenian philosopher whose death established the classical paradigm of political obligation that Bosanquet reinterprets through the general will.'

relations.label: Chapter IV: The Authority of the State

memes.label: Sovereignty
memes.description: Proudhon's critique of sovereignty as the rule of will over reason — whether exercised by a king or by the people — and its connection to the absolutism he identifies in property.

memes.label: Sparta
memes.description: 'Sparta as discussed by Bosanquet—an example of the ancient Greek city-state contrasted with Athens in the history of political thought.'

relations.label: Chapter I: The Rise of the Modern Theory of the State

memes.label: State
memes.description: The State is the central political concept in Bosanquet's philosophical theory — a real unity, a moral organism, and the embodiment of the general will.

relations.label: Chapter VI: The State and the General Will

memes.label: T.H. Green
memes.description: 'T.H. Green (1836–1882), British idealist philosopher whose theory of positive freedom and the common good directly influenced Bosanquetʼs political thought.'

relations.label: Lectures on the Principles of Political Obligation
Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power
Chapter V: Liberty and the General Will

memes.label: T.H. Huxley
memes.description: Thomas Henry Huxley (1825–1895), British biologist and philosopher whose Evolution and Ethics Bosanquet engages with critically on the relationship between nature, self-assertion, and social restraint.

memes.label: Thomas Hobbes
memes.description: 'Thomas Hobbes (1588–1679), English philosopher whose Leviathan established the modern theory of sovereignty that Bosanquet critically engages and transforms.'

relations.label: Chapter III: The Problem of State-Power

memes.label: Wartburg Festival
memes.description: The 1817 gathering of German nationalist students at Wartburg Castle, discussed by Bosanquet as part of the political context surrounding Hegel's Philosophy of Right.

memes.label: Will
memes.description: In Bosanquet's theory, Will is the fundamental psychological and ethical category — the rational self-determining principle that finds its fullest expression in the general will of the State.

relations.label: Chapter VI: The State and the General Will

memes.label: William Wallace
memes.description: William Wallace (1844–1897), Scottish philosopher and British Idealist, translator of Hegel's Philosophy of Mind, whose essays Bosanquet cites as foundational to the understanding of natural rights within idealist political theory.
